title: Quantum Spin Liquid State of a Dual-Species Atomic Array on Kagome Lattice
authors: Ahmed M. Farouk, Ilya I. Beterov, Ghadeer Suliman, Junxi Chen, Igor I. Ryabtsev
abstract: Dual-species arrays of ultracold neutral atoms have recently attracted increased interest due to the ability to independently control different atomic species and tune the interatomic interactions. This capability provides additional flexibility essential for both quantum computing and quantum simulation. In this work we theoretically investigate a quantum spin liquid (QSL) state to be simulated on a programmable quantum simulator based on a dual-species atomic array, arranged on a Kagome lattice. The Kagome lattice is formed by corner sharing triangles. This specific spatial arrangement enhances the competing interactions between atoms and is often considered as a model for realising QSL states. When the atoms are excited into Rydberg states, long-range interactions result in Rydberg blockade. The geometric frustration of the Kagome lattice, combined with the Rydberg blockade, drives the system into exotic phases with topological order and long-range entanglement. To drive an array into the QSL state, we use a sweep-quench-sweep protocol, when the atoms are quasiadiabatically excited into Rydberg state with individually controlled detuning from the resonance for each atomic species. The filling fraction, indicating emergence of a QSL state, is represented by a density of Rydberg excitations. We identified the conditions required for QSL state in a dual-species array with non-uniform interaction energies. We calculated the correlation length and studied the mutual information as a function of the size of the subset of the system. The existence of a topological order was proved by estimating the Kitaev-Preskill topological quantum entanglement entropy.
summary: This paper theoretically explores how a dual-species Rydberg atom array on a Kagome lattice can be driven into a Quantum Spin Liquid state. By using a specific laser-tuning protocol, the authors show that the system can exhibit topological order and long-range entanglement. This work provides a framework for using different atomic species to control many-body physics in quantum simulators.
main_problem: Investigating the emergence and properties of a Quantum Spin Liquid (QSL) state in a dual-species (Rb and Cs) Rydberg atom array arranged on a Kagome lattice.
main_result: The study identifies specific detuning and quenching conditions to achieve a QSL state and proves the existence of topological order via Kitaev-Preskill topological quantum entanglement entropy.
method: Numerical simulations using the Bloqade.jl Julia package, employing a sweep-quench-sweep protocol and analyzing correlation length, mutual information, and entanglement entropy.
model_or_system: A programmable quantum simulator consisting of a dual-species (Rb and Cs) ultracold neutral atom array on a Kagome lattice, governed by a Hamiltonian with Rydberg blockade and long-range van der Waals interactions.
key_observables: Rydberg density (filling fraction), correlation length, mutual information, and Kitaev-Preskill topological quantum entanglement entropy (TQEE).
important_parameters: Lattice spacing (a), detuning ratio (nu), detuning parameter (Delta Rb/Omega), quenching time (tq), and interatomic interaction energies (V_ij).
main_assumptions: The system is studied within the Rydberg blockade regime using a truncated subspace approach to manage Hilbert space dimensionality.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the Kagome lattice spatial configuration; Fig 2 plots excitation configuration probabilities; Fig 4 shows mutual information scaling; Fig 5 and 6 illustrate the evolution of topological entanglement entropy and its peaks at QSL boundaries.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the dual-species Kagome platform, defines the Hamiltonian and excitation protocol, analyzes configuration probabilities and correlation lengths, and concludes with the quantification of topological order through entanglement entropy.
why_interesting: It demonstrates how heteronuclear interaction asymmetry can be exploited to engineer exotic topological phases like the Z2 spin liquid, offering a new pathway for preparing highly entangled states in Rydberg simulators.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) A dual-species array of 87Rb and 133Cs atoms shaped as Kagome lattice. It consists of hexagons, each hexagon is surrounded by the triangles consisting of three atoms. The lattice spacing constant a is the distance between any of two atoms in the corner-sharing triangle. The distance between two atoms located on the two neighboring sides of any hexagon’s vertex is 2a. Rb and Cs atoms are shown as blue and orange circles, respectively. (b) The interaction energy between Rb and Cs atoms both being excited to Rydberg state |r⟩= |81S1/2, mj = 1/2⟩as a function of interatomic distance R, indicating the dipole-dipole and van der Waals regimes. FIG. 3. The calculation of correlation length ξ. (a) The density-density correlation function between atoms g(2)(r) as a function of the interatomic distance r. It shows the amplitude decay of the correlations as the distance increases. (b) The extracted value of the correlation length ξ, calculated as a function of the dimensionless parameter ∆Rb/Ω0 for different configurations of the system. The solid-red and dashed- red curves represent the cases when ν = 1.5 and ν = 0.1, respectively, for a = 4.0 µm, and for the quenching time tq = 0.25. There are two atoms, which are not included in any of the subsets. The subsets A and B (B and C) have shared-edges, while A and C does not have any shared edges.
